channel.title: World news | The Guardian
channel.description: Latest World news news, comment and analysis from the Guardian, the world's leading liberal voice
channel.copyright: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026
channel.dc:rights: Guardian News and Media Limited or its affiliated companies. All rights reserved. 2026

item.title: Two Russian men jailed in Angola for terrorism and spying
item.description:
The men were convicted over alleged plan to stage coup and organise deadly demonstrations over fuel prices last year

Two Russian men have been found guilty by an Angolan court of terrorism and spying after being accused of planning a coup and helping to organise deadly demonstrations in the southern African country last year.

Lev Lakshtanov and Igor Ratchin were sentenced to eight and 11 years in jail respectively. They had denied the charges, claiming they were in Angola for cultural and business activities.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de Angola
Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de Angola
Photograph: Ministerio do Interior/Governo de Angola

item.dc:creator: Rachel Savage in Johannesburg and agencies

item.title: MoD cancels army training in Kenya over powers to prosecute British soldiers
item.description:
September exercise called off after Kenya raises concerns over funding and jurisdiction for criminal offences

The British army has been forced to cancel training for its troops in Kenya after the Kenyan government refused to ratify a bilateral defence agreement.

While the UK government wanted to renew the existing agreement, Kenyan lawmakers had sought changes to give local authorities greater powers to hold British soldiers accountable for any offences committed in the country.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Al-Othman North of England correspondent

item.title: Health worker monitored for Ebola in London hospital after working in DRC
item.description:
UK resident was evacuated from Democratic Republic of the Congo where there is an outbreak of the disease

A humanitarian worker is being monitored at a specialist hospital in London after potential exposure to the Ebola virus.

The UK resident was medically evacuated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) where the worker had been treating patients with the disease, the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Nadeem Badshah

item.title: Healthy diet too expensive for one in three people globally, UN report finds
item.description:
Global hunger declined for the third year in a row in 2025 but experts call for action to bring down costs of fruit, vegetables and dairy as report shows 2.69bn people can’t afford to eat well

One in three people cannot afford to eat a healthy diet, a new report has found, prompting calls for urgent action on food affordability.

There are 2.69 billion people unable to afford a diverse diet that meets their energy and nutrient needs, and it will take “immense” amounts of work to achieve the global target of zero hunger by 2030, the annual report from five UN agencies warned.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Kat Lay Global health correspondent

item.title: São Tomé and Príncipe heads to polls in tense presidential election
item.description:
Incumbent Carlos Vila Nova hopes to defeat his former party and secure second term as independent

Voters in São Tomé and Príncipe go to the polls for a presidential election on Sunday as one of Africa’s least populous countries seeks to burnish its democratic credentials.

According to the National Election Commission, about 142,000 people are registered to vote in the tiny African state’s elections, approximately 15% of whom live in the diaspora.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Eromo Egbejule in Abidjan

item.title: Mayor shot dead in Mexico months after surviving assassination attempt
item.description:
Valentín Lavín Romero is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in 2024

A mayor was shot dead on Wednesday in the Mexican state of Morelos after having survived a previous assassination attempt earlier this year. He is the 14th mayor to be murdered since Claudia Sheinbaum became president in October 2024.

Valentín Lavín Romero, who was from the Green party, an ally of the ruling leftwing Morena party, was wounded at the end of January when hitmen shot him at a petrol station on a motorway.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Thomas Graham

item.title: Canada cancels joint bridge-opening celebration with US amid trade tensions
item.description:
Trump’s 50% tariff threat means there will be no ribbon-cutting celebration of Gordie Howe International Bridge

A planned joint US-Canada ceremony on Friday celebrating the opening of the Gordie Howe International Bridge connecting Detroit and Windsor, Ontario, is no longer expected to take place after Donald Trump announced a 50% tariff on most Canadian goods.

In announcing the tariffs, the president accused Canada of unfairly discriminating against US automobiles, alcohol and dairy products, escalating tensions with one of the United States’ closest allies.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Olivia Bowden and agencies

item.title: Nicaragua’s congress announces ‘work plan’ to suspend elections
item.description:
US secretary of state says international community should ‘join forces’ against authoritarian Ortega regime

Nicaragua’s congress, controlled by the country’s long-serving president, Daniel Ortega, has announced a “work plan” to implement the authoritarian leader’s order that the country should no longer hold elections.

The UN and the US strongly condemned Sunday’s announcement by Ortega, who has been in power since 2007 and, after a controversial constitutional change last year, now governs alongside his wife, Rosario Murillo, as “co-president”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Tiago Rogero South America correspondent

item.title: Sperm whales change ‘vowel’ sounds when boats are nearby, scientists discover
item.description:
Researchers listening off the Caribbean island of Dominica found variations in the mammals’ audible clicks when marine traffic was close

Sperm whales produce a specific pattern of clicking noises when they hear the noise of boats, researchers have found. The pattern is so distinct that the scientists who identified it said they can now use it to predict when ships are nearby.

Researchers from Project Ceti (the Cetacean Translation Initiative), a non-profit research organisation working to decode sperm whale communication, spent four years tuning in on the calls of 15 sperm whales off the coast of Dominica.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Emma Bryce

item.title: Trump announces new tariffs on generic drugs to take effect in 2028 – as it happened
item.description:
This live blog is now closed.

  • Sign up for US Breaking News email

US gas prices have continued to rise since the conflict with Iran resumed this month. According to the American Automobile Association (AAA), a gallon of fuel is now just over $4 – almost a dollar more than a year ago.

It comes as Brent Crude, the international oil benchmark, is now up by almost 2% to $90.92 a barrel.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Robert Mackey, Shrai Popat, Maya Yang and Tom Ambrose

item.title: Total solar eclipse books out hotels two years in advance in New Zealand city
item.description:
Dunedin will be the focal point, having last experienced such an eclipse in 1163 – before any humans reached New Zealand

Hotels are booking out two years in advance in the New Zealand city of Dunedin as it prepares for the region’s first total solar eclipse in more than 850 years.

At 4.17pm on 22 July 2028, the moon will pass in front of the sun, casting a roughly 100km-wide shadowy band across parts of southern New Zealand. Everywhere within that band – from tourist hotspot Queenstown in the west to Dunedin city in the east – will be plunged into daytime darkness for 2 minutes and 51 seconds.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Eva Corlett in Wellington

item.title: British woman jailed in Hong Kong for blackmail after accusing banker of rape
item.description:
Isabel Rose, 26, sentenced to six years for blackmail and perverting course of public justice over her allegations against British man

A British woman who reported being raped to police in Hong Kong has been sentenced to six years in prison for blackmail and perverting the course of public justice.

Isabel Rose, 26, from south-east London, travelled to Hong Kong in early 2024 to visit a British banker she had met while travelling in south-east Asia.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Danny Vincent

item.title: Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal
item.description:
Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Penny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week.

The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders against placing a toll on the strait of Hormuz after both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sarah Basford Canales and Luca Ittimani

item.title: Michelin-starred restaurant owner in South Korea faces jail over ant dessert
item.description:
Prosecutors seek a one-year sentence and heavy fine after chef garnished dish with insects not approved for consumption

Prosecutors in South Korea have asked a court to sentence the owner of a Michelin-starred restaurant to one year in prison for serving a dessert topped with ants, an insect not approved for consumption under local food safety law.

The request, made on Monday, also sought a 20m won (£10,108) fine for the company that runs the restaurant, Yonhap news agency reported.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea
Photograph: Ministry of Food and Drug Safety Korea

item.dc:creator: Raphael Rashid

item.title: Japan records its first ‘cruelly hot day’ as cities swelter in 40-degree heat
item.description:
In April Japan coined new term kokushobi for days above 40C as country advises workers to wear shorts to the office to cope with stifling heat

Japan logged its first official kokushobi or “cruelly hot day” on Tuesday since the new term for when temperatures top 40 degrees Celsius was introduced in April. The Japan Meteorological Agency coined the new designation to warn the public of the heat dangers of days that were once freak weather events but have now become more common.

The central Japan cities of Gujo and Tajimi in Gifu prefecture, along with Toyota in Aichi prefecture, all hit 40C. Gujo’s 40C and Toyota’s 40.1C were all-time highs recorded at their respective weather stations.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Gavin Blair in Tokyo

item.title: Pauline Hanson blames ‘fake news’ media for her bad press. But serious leaders have to own their actions
item.description:
In a fortnight of rolling blunders, the One Nation leader digs deep into the Trump playbook of blame-shifting

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

There’s apparently only one thing to blame for Pauline Hanson’s string of bad headlines in recent weeks, from her decision to pal around with the far-right convicted criminal Tommy Robinson to her description of domestic violence on Wednesday as a “two-way street”.

According to the One Nation leader, it’s of course not Hanson herself who is to blame.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Josh Butler

item.title: Personal and banking details among customer data stolen in Origin Energy hack
item.description:
Hackers access Australian customers’ names, addresses, dates of birth, phone numbers and some bank account details, company says

  • Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Origin Energy customers’ addresses, phone numbers and partial bank account data have been accessed in a hack, the company has confirmed.

The firm has 4.8m customer accounts in Australia, providing electricity, fossil gas, LPG and internet services to homes and businesses.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Luca Ittimani

item.title: Pauline Hanson says domestic violence prevents police from dealing with ‘real crime’ in unearthed interview
item.description:
One Nation leader labels family violence leave ‘ridiculous’ and decries ‘woke’ support of women’s rights in November podcast

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Pauline Hanson has described paid leave for those experiencing family and domestic violence as “ridiculous”, and suggested the issue is taking police away from “real crime”, calling for the family law system to be “wound back”.

In an unearthed interview, the One Nation leader said the advancements of women’s rights in the family law system over the decades had become “broken” because they favoured women and were now “suppressing the males all the time”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sarah Basford Canales

item.title: Former spy refused visa to give evidence at royal commission – as it happened
item.description:
This blog is now closed.

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Self-reported domestic violence incidents declining in NSW but deaths and hospitalisations fail to fall

New data shows self-reported domestic violence incidents in New South Wales have declined, but the number of women and children murdered or hospitalised in domestic and family violence incidents over the past eight years has not significantly budged.

The decline in self-reported violence is significant because it reflects underlying levels of violence in the community, not just what is reported to police. It suggests that, despite rising reports of domestic violence to police, domestic violence in the community is not increasing and may be trending down.

There’s a strong perception that violence against women is rising, which is understandable given increases in police-recorded incidents, heightened media attention and greater public awareness.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Achol Arok and Caitlin Cassidy

item.title: Husic says Labor ‘allergic to dissent’ as he breaks ranks on key government positions at party conference
item.description:
Criticism comes as prime minister frames unity as point of difference to political opponents, saying ALP chooses ‘progress over protest’

  • Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Outspoken Labor MP Ed Husic claims the party has become “allergic” to dissent and dispute, warning the unity on show at its tightly controlled national conference could backfire.

The former industry minister broke ranks with the government on the first day of Labor’s 50th party conference to support a gas export tax, endorse Indigenous truth-telling and press for more criticism of Israel’s conduct in Gaza.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Dan Jervis-Bardy Chief political correspondent

item.title: Thousands flee wildfires in France as temperatures reach extreme levels in southern Europe
item.description:
Some parts of Mediterranean Sea 5C hotter than normal, with air temperatures above 44C expected in Spain

  • Europe live – latest updates

Brutal heat torching southern Europe has forced more than 20,000 people to flee wildfires in France as a marine heatwave scalds the Mediterranean and researchers warn that “Dantesque” scenarios cannot be ruled out as infernos worsen.

A “particularly intense” wildfire on France’s Atlantic coast prompted a series of evacuations, including thousands of tourists from campsites and other holiday accommodation, as firefighters struggled to control the blaze.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jon Henley and Ajit Niranjan

item.title: Ousted Ukrainian defence minister refuses any other role in Zelenskyy’s government – as it happened
item.description:
Mykhailo Fedorov says only in his former post could he ‘combat procurement corruption and complete the army’s transformation’

EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas has offered a bit more detail on the new package of sanctions, saying they are “the largest round in four years,” and will “hit Putin where it hurts most.”

In a statement, she said:

“We have agreed on the 21st sanctions package against Russia.

It delivers sweeping measures targeting Moscow’s financial system, its military-industrial complex and energy sector, that keep Russia’s war economy running.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jakub Krupa

item.title: Owner of Viking ship used in The Odyssey says studio has not paid bill for damage
item.description:
Swedish nonprofit alleges cost to repair replica was $6,000 but Universal – which made $264m on film’s opening weekend – has not stumped up

The Swedish owners of a Viking longship replica that features in the Hollywood blockbuster The Odyssey say they feel “forgotten” after film-makers allegedly returned the vessel damaged and failed to foot the bill for repairs.

In nearly three decades at sea, the warship Glad av Gillberga – based on a wreck from around 1040 found in Denmark’s Roskilde fjord – has successfully sailed the North Sea, the Baltic and even voyaged to Canada.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Miranda Bryant Nordic correspondent

item.title: Murdoch and Musk in the crosshairs at Venice film festival
item.description:
Danny Boyle’s drama about the acquisition of the Sun and a four-hour Elon Musk documentary will premiere alongside new films from Martin McDonagh and Werner Herzog – but only one female director has a movie in competition

The Venice film festival promises to take a swing at media moguls of the print and digital era this year, with Trainspotting director Danny Boyle’s new film about Rupert Murdoch’s acquisition of the Sun and a new documentary about Elon Musk included in the lineup of the films for the festival’s 83rd edition.

In Boyle’s Ink, which will open the festival on the Lido on 2 September, Australian actor Guy Pearce plays compatriot newspaper tycoon Murdoch, whose purchase of the Sun in 1969 sensationalised the media landscape and transformed public life in Britain. The script is written by Olivier award-winning playwright and screenwriter James Graham (Dear England, Brexit: The Uncivil War).

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Philip Oltermann European culture editor

item.title: Risk of Parkinson’s disease may increase with prolonged exposure to road traffic noise
item.description:
Pioneering study linking noise pollution to Parkinson’s finds 3% increase in risk for every 11.5dB rise in noise levels

Long-term exposure to road traffic noise is associated with a higher risk of Parkinson’s disease, the biggest study of its kind has found.

Parkinson’s disease is a progressive disorder in which parts of the brain become damaged over time, affecting movement and balance.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Ushika Kidd

item.title: Trump considering ‘massive attack’ on Iran, says report – Middle East crisis live
item.description:
US president tells Axios ‘they haven’t received enough pain yet’ and he is ‘close to a decision’

Earlier in the US day, before Iran’s Revolutionary Guard said it had stopped three oil tankers from passing through the strait of Hormuz, Donald Trump issued this threat on his Truth Social platform:

From this point forward, any time the Islamic Republic of Iran shoots at a ship in the Strait of Hormuz, whether it be by Missile, Rocket, Drone, or any other device or weapon, the United States will bomb and destroy ONE BRIDGE OR POWER PLANT, including those located next to, or in, the Capital City of Tehran.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Lucy Campbell (now); Vivian Ho, Taz Ali, Siraj Datoo and Adam Fulton (earlier)

item.title: Trump says he is close to decision on largest-yet attack on Iran
item.description:
Escalation would follow oil passing $100 a barrel again and Houthi strikes on Saudi tankers in the Red Sea

The US president, Donald Trump, has said he is close to deciding whether to launch “a massive” attack” against Iran, on a scale larger than strikes already witnessed during the months-long conflict with Tehran.

Speaking to the Axios news site Trump said: “I am considering a massive attack. Bigger than ever before. I am close to making a decision. We are all set for it.”

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Peter Beaumont Senior international correspondent

item.title: Israeli security minister joins Jewish extremists as they storm al-Aqsa mosque compound
item.description:
Far-right minister Itamar Ben-Gvir was among thousands who gathered at Jerusalem’s most sensitive holy site

Israel’s far-right minister of national security, Itamar Ben-Gvir, joined thousands of Jewish extremists who stormed Jerusalem’s most sensitive holy site on Thursday.

Escorted by armed police, they prayed, waved the Israeli flag and sang the national anthem in a provocative display of political power.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Emma Graham-Harrison

item.title: Oil passes $100 a barrel again and shares slide as Middle East conflict escalates
item.description:
Sharp rise in oil price reflects fears Houthi militias could strangle Saudi exports as US-Iran fighting intensifies

  • How the Bab al-Mandab blockade threat pushed oil back above $100

The oil price broke above $100 a barrel for the first time in two months as a fresh escalation of the Middle East conflict threatens to compound disruption to global oil supplies.

The benchmark oil price rose sharply on Thursday, from $95 the day before, amid fears that Yemen’s Houthi militia could strangle Saudi oil exports through the Red Sea while US-Iran tensions over oil flows through the strait of Hormuz intensify.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jillian Ambrose, Joanna Partridge and Richard Partington

item.title: Dubai offers £600 rewards to residents who lure friends to visit as tourist numbers plummet
item.description:
Residents eligible for dining and hotel packages if they can persuade family and friends to city spurned by visitors amid Iran war

  • Middle East crisis – live updates

Dubai is offering to pay residents who attract family and friends to visit the emirate in an effort to revive tourist numbers that have plummeted since the city was drawn into the US-Israel war on Iran.

The Dubai Invite scheme, an initiative of the department of economy and tourism, allows residents of the United Arab Emirates to nominate friends and relatives and then to receive benefits worth more than 3,000 UAE dirham (about £610) if they visit.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Natasha May

item.title: ‘Cockroach’ protests: India’s Modi promises to punish officials over exam leaks
item.description:
PM says ‘those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared’ as scandal spirals into widespread anger

Narendra Modi has vowed to set up “fast-track courts” to speed up the punishment of officials accused of leaking examination papers, as the Indian prime minister seeks to quell a youth-led “cockroach” protest movement that has rocked Delhi and posed one of the biggest challenges to his government.

In his first public remarks on the movement, Modi posted on X: “Nothing is more important than the welfare and future of our youth … Those who try to harm the future of our youth will not be spared.”

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Amrit Dhillon in Delhi

item.title: Opposition parties shut down Indian parliament in solidarity with ‘cockroach’ protests
item.description:
Proceedings suspended as politicians call for Modi government to address police violence and education scandals

India’s opposition parties have shut down parliament in a protest calling for the resignation of the education minister, in solidarity with the new youth-led “cockroach” movement.

Opposition politicians, led by the Congress party leader Rahul Gandhi, gathered outside the parliament building in Delhi on Wednesday, many dressed in black and waving placards calling on Dharmendra Pradhan to quit.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: India’s Cockroach movement protesters show no sign of backing down
item.description:
Thousands of people again on the streets of Delhi calling for education reforms despite police violence on Monday

Taru had come to the protest prepared this time. In one hand was an anti-government poster and in the other was a bicycle helmet. “We need to have protection in case Delhi police beat us up again,” said the 25-year-old, who stood among her friends. “We are not scared of them and we won’t run away, no matter what they do. We’ll stay here till they listen.”

On Tuesday, central Delhi still thronged with thousands of protesters, as an unprecedented expression of dissent against the powerful ruling Narendra Modi government showed no sign of abating.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: Rescuers in Afghanistan search for missing and dead after flash floods destroy homes
item.description:
National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in eastern province of Nuristan

Rescue crews in Afghanistan are searching for survivors and bodies among the rubble of houses destroyed by devastating flash floods that authorities said had killed nearly two dozen people and left more than 100 people missing.

The Afghanistan national disaster management authority said on Tuesday that 23 people had died, 80 had been injured and more than 100 were missing after floods struck the eastern province of Nuristan on Monday, causing an undetermined number of buildings to collapse. The provincial capital city of Parun was among the areas affected, authorities said.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Associated Press in Kabul

item.title: Tens of thousands of Cockroach movement protesters defy police with Delhi march
item.description:
Youth-led group calls on education minister to quit in one of India’s largest anti-government rallies in years

Tens of thousands of protesters have defied police orders and descended on Delhi to demand the resignation of India’s education minister in one of the largest anti-government demonstrations in years.

A huge crowd, largely made up of students and gen Z-ers, filled the streets in the heart of India’s capital from early Monday morning shouting anti-government slogans. Hundreds had already camped out overnight.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ellis-Petersen in Delhi

item.title: Business rates cut for pubs, clubs and music venues in England only ‘a small move’, say critics
item.description:
Andy Burnham faces calls to be more ambitious with hospitality businesses calling for VAT cuts and help for hotels and restaurants

Pubs, clubs and live music venues in England will receive a 20% discount on their business rates bills as part of a £100m aid package unveiled by Andy Burnham but the prime minister immediately faced calls to be more ambitious.

Burnham said the measure, aimed at supporting the high street and easing cost of living pressures, would be fully funded, including through a review of relief for businesses deemed not to make a positive contribution to communities, such as vape shops.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Pippa Crerar and Rob Davies

item.title: Lobbying firm pressed for details about payments to journalists for favourable stories
item.description:
MPs and others condemn behaviour of CT Group, which offered cash for positive coverage of clients

A political lobbying firm secretly paying journalists for articles favourable to its clients is facing calls to reveal full details of the payments, after the practice was denounced as a dangerous “attack on democracy”.

MPs, senior lobbyists and transparency campaigners all condemned the behaviour of CT Group, the firm co-founded by the campaign strategist Lynton Crosby, an election guru previously hired by both David Cameron and Boris Johnson.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Michael Savage Media editor

item.title: ‘Customers prefer AI chatbots,’ says British Gas owner as 1,300 call centre jobs axed
item.description:
CEO Chris O’Shea defends Centrica’s plans as it reports rise in retail profits as it focuses on bigger margins

The owner of British Gas has claimed that most households would rather speak with an AI chatbot than deal with the company’s staff as it prepares to cut 1,300 jobs from its call centres.

Centrica, the supplier’s FTSE 100 owner, plans to cut 800 jobs as the company carries out a “targeted deployment of AI tools”, on top of the 500 cutsit confirmed last month.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Jillian Ambrose Energy correspondent

item.title: UK politics: Burnham pauses early prisoner release scheme due to start in September – as it happened
item.description:
New PM says no prisoners will be released under the policy until there is an urgent review of scheme

Kemi Badenoch is speaking in central London at the moment and is expected to say that Andy Burnham is “still thinking like the mayor of Manchester”.

“Going by what he’s announced so far, Andy Burnham’s ambitions for Britain are too small,” she was expected to say.

My message to British businesses is quite simple: to British businesses, to British innovators, to British investors: I will back you as your Chancellor, and I’ll back you in financial services, in technology, in retail, in industry, in all parts of the economy.

I’m just as concerned about the cost of business as I am about the cost of living.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Hayden Vernon (now); Tom Ambrose and Vivian Ho (earlier)

item.title: Mission to ‘eradicate scourge of drowning’ after deaths surge across England
item.description:
Strategy will tackle ‘public health disaster’ that claims more lives than fire, flooding or knife crime

A national strategy to address the “public health disaster” of drowning has been launched in England – hours after two women died at an Essex beach, the latest in a surge of water-related tragedies.

Among the measures being considered are: providing every child in England with water safety education and swimming lessons; establishing how climate change can increase risks; and media campaigns to inform bystanders how to respond to a potential drowning.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Sammy Gecsoyler

item.title: White House says if Saudi Arabia does not join Abraham accords, nuclear deal ‘is off’ – US politics live
item.description:
Karoline Leavitt insisted nuclear deal is ‘contingent’ on condition revealed by Trump in a social post

  • US House passes second war powers resolution

  • Sign up for the Breaking News US email

By a vote of 214-208 lawmakers in the House passed a war powers resolution to curb the Trump administration’s military action in Iran, ahead of the lower chamber’s August reccess.

Four Republicans, Brian Fitzpatrick, Warren Davidson, Tom Barrett and Thomas Massie, voted yes, alongside all Democrats.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Shrai Popat (now); Lucy Campbell and Vivian Ho (earlier)

item.title: White House trails announcement on tariffs amid reports of new duties
item.description:
Trump officials say they will announce next phase of US trade policy as 10% global tariffs are due to expire on Friday

Donald Trump’s administration will unveil the latest stage of his aggressive trade policy on Thursday, the White House has said, amid reports the US president is preparing a fresh wave of sweeping tariffs.

Trump’s tariffs policy suffered a damaging blow in February when the US supreme court ruled that many of those tariffs were illegal. While the US replaced those tariffs with a 10% tariff regime on much of the world, these are due to expire on Friday.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Shrai Popat in Washington

item.title: 5.0-magnitude earthquake strikes Texas panhandle
item.description:
No injuries or deaths were reported from early-morning temblor whose epicenter was 68 miles from Amarillo

  • Sign up for the Breaking News US newsletter email

A 5.0-magnitude earthquake struck the Texas panhandle, the state’s northernmost region, on Thursday, marking the strongest quake to hit the area since 1900.

The earthquake struck at around 5.21am at a depth of 3.5 miles (5.7km), according to the US Geological Survey (USGS). Its epicenter was about 27.8 miles south-east of the city of Spearman and approximately 68 miles from Amarillo.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Maya Yang

item.title: Tropical Storm Bertha heads for Texas after Louisiana landfall
item.description:
Forecasters warn of US coastal flooding, heavy rain and dangerous surf as the storm tracks west

Tropical Storm Bertha made landfall in Louisiana on Wednesday afternoon, and is moving westward toward the Texas coast, where it is expected to reach later on Thursday, bringing rainfall, winds and rising water levels.

In a Thursday morning update, the US National Hurricane Center (NHC) said that Bertha was moving west and that the center of the tropical storm was expected to move near or along the Louisiana and upper Texas coasts over the next several hours before moving inland over Texas on Thursday evening.

Continue reading...

media:content.media:credit: Photograph: David Grunfeld/The Times-Picayune/The New Orleans Advocate/AP
Photograph: David Grunfeld/The Times-Picayune/The New Orleans Advocate/AP
Photograph: David Grunfeld/The Times-Picayune/The New Orleans Advocate/AP

item.dc:creator: Anna Betts

item.title: At least two people missing in West Virginia after torrential rain causes flash floods
item.description:
State’s governor had declared a state of emergency for all 55 counties, and deployed 500 national guard members

Patrick Morrisey, the West Virginia governor, said at least two people were unaccounted for in Upshur county, after torrential rain caused flash floods in parts of the state.

Speaking at a news conference, Morrisey said that during search-and-recovery operations state police found an overturned vehicle in Upshur county believed to be “connected to two individuals who were reported missing”.

Continue reading...
item.dc:creator: Anna Betts
